My open-ended straight draw gives me approximately 32% equity if I see both the turn and the river. Given the pot odds (350 to win 1300), I have a clear call to see the turn. Raising is also an option as a semi-bluff, but calling allows me to realize my equity more cheaply while keeping the pot manageable against a stack I significantly cover.

On the turn card 5h, the board is 9s-2d-Td-5h. I still hold an open-ended straight draw with 8d-7s. As the big blind acting first post-flop, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor, Claude. This allows me to see how he reacts to the turn card. If he checks behind, I get a free card to try and hit my straight on the river. If he bets, I will evaluate the pot odds to decide on a call, as my equity remains high with 8 outs.

Betting QT for value on the turn in heads-up play. Flash has checked twice, indicating weakness or a draw. My top pair with top kicker is very likely the best hand on this dry board. The 5H on the turn didn't improve Flash's likely range significantly. A turn bet of 450 extracts value from weaker hands, protects against draws completing for free, and maintains my aggressive position. This is a standard value bet sizing that's appropriate for my stack size and the pot.

On the turn card 5h, the board is 9s-2d-Td-5h. I currently have an open-ended straight draw with 8d-7s (needing a 6 or a Jack to complete). Claude has bet 450 into a pot of 1300 (before the bet), making the total pot 1750. I need to call 450 to see the river. This gives me pot odds of approximately 3.9:1 (or 20.4% equity needed). With 8 outs to a straight, my probability of hitting on the river is approximately 17.4%. While I am slightly below the direct pot odds, the implied odds are significant given Claude's remaining stack and the potential to extract more value if I hit my straight. Therefore, calling is the most profitable play to realize my equity.

On the river card 2s, the board is 9s-2d-Td-5h-2s. My open-ended straight draw has missed, leaving me with only eight-high. As the first player to act on the river after check-calling the previous streets, leading out with a bet (donk betting) into the pre-flop aggressor is rarely a winning play, especially when the board does not significantly improve my perceived range or weaken Claude's. My most strategic option is to check and see how Claude reacts. If Claude checks behind, I will lose the pot at showdown to almost any hand. If Claude bets, I will likely have to fold as a bluff-raise is high-risk against a player who has shown aggression on every street.

Betting top pair top kicker for value on the river in heads-up play. Flash has checked three streets, indicating a weak hand or missed draw. My QT gives me a pair of tens with a queen kicker, which is very likely the best hand on this 9-2-10-5-2 board. A river bet of 900 chips (roughly 40% of the 2200 pot) extracts value from Flash's weaker hands while maintaining appropriate bet sizing relative to my 9850 stack. This is a standard value extraction play when opponent shows repeated weakness.

On the river card 2s, the board is 9s-2d-Td-5h-2s. I have missed my open-ended straight draw and am left with only eight-high, which has no showdown value. Claude has been the aggressor on every street, including a pre-flop raise and bets on the flop, turn, and now the river. His bet of 900 into a pot of 2200 represents a strong range or a determined bluff. Given that I have check-called the entire way, my range is capped and mostly consists of draws that have now missed. Raising as a bluff is extremely risky against a player who has shown such consistent aggression and still has a significant stack. Folding is the most prudent and disciplined play to preserve my large stack lead.

I am on the button with K3o. In a heads-up situation, a King-high hand is strong enough to open for a raise. I have a significant chip lead over Claude. Standard play here is to raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

Folding J2o from the big blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker and I'm out of position against an aggressive opponent. Despite reasonable pot odds (2:1), the positional disadvantage and weak hand strength make this unprofitable. Near the end of the tournament (hand 136/150) with a decent stack, I should wait for better spots.

Heads-up position play from the button with 7-Jack offsuit. Although this is not a premium hand, proper heads-up strategy requires raising a wide range from the button to put pressure on the big blind. Flash is relatively short-stacked in this matchup now (relative to my position equity), and maintaining aggression is critical. A 3x big blind raise (to 300) is standard aggressive play that applies maximum leverage from position. The hand plays reasonably well in position post-flop against Flash's likely checking or calling range, and I expect to win significant pots when Flash folds. With diminishing stack sizes, I need to maintain an aggressive posture rather than allow passive play.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s4s. Claude has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. While 74s is a speculative hand, it has decent playability in a heads-up situation, especially since I have a significant chip lead over Claude (27950 vs 11650). However, 74s is near the bottom of a defending range even against a standard 3x raise. Given that Claude has been fairly active and often folds to pressure or post-flop aggression, I could call to see a flop, but folding is also a disciplined option to avoid bloated pots with weak holdings. Considering the pot odds (200 to call for a total pot of 600), I am getting 2:1, but the hand strength is marginal. I will choose to fold this time and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

I am on the button with 8d 6d, a solid suited connector in a heads-up situation. My opponent, Claude, is in the big blind. I have been playing aggressively pre-flop, and this hand is well within a standard raising range to put pressure on the big blind. A standard raise of 3x (300 total) is appropriate here to build the pot and potentially take it down immediately or see a flop with a hand that has good playability.

7-3 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. While Flash has shown aggression throughout this session and could be raising light on the button, I'm still holding an extremely weak hand that performs poorly against any reasonable range. Even with decent pot odds (need ~25% equity), 7-3o doesn't have the equity to justify calling against a wide raising range. From the big blind against aggressive opponents, a tight folding range with weak holdings is more profitable long-term than trying to exploit their aggression with a hand this poor. I'll wait for better opportunities where I have more hand strength or better position.

Heads-up play from the button with 9-10 offsuit. This is a playable hand that's above average for this position. With a strong raise equity and only 50 chips to call to complete my small blind, I should be aggressive and take the initiative rather than just calling. A standard raise to 200 (3x the big blind) puts pressure on Flash and allows me to win the hand preflop or take control with position if called. Given Flash's aggression throughout the session and my need to maintain my stack with 12 hands remaining, taking down pots with aggression is important.

In Hand #139, I'm in the Big Blind with Ten of Spades and Four of Clubs. Claude has raised to 200 from the Button (Small Blind). The pot is 300 and it costs me only 100 to call. While T4o is a weak hand, the price offered (3-to-1) is quite attractive in a heads-up situation, especially since I am the current chip leader and have seen Claude be somewhat aggressive but foldable on later streets. However, T4o is near the bottom of a defending range and out of position. Given the stage of the game (Hand 139 of 150) and my significant lead, I can afford to see a flop cheaply to protect my big blind and potentially outplay him if the board coordinates, but a fold is also standard to avoid being trapped out of position with a marginal holding.

On a flop of 3s-4h-8h, I have middle pair (Fours) with a Ten kicker. This is a marginal made hand. Being out of position against Claude, the standard play is to check and evaluate his reaction. I can likely call a standard continuation bet to see if the hand improves or if Claude is simply betting with air or a draw.

Flash's flop check is a weakness indicator, especially given Flash's aggressive pattern throughout the session. With 12 hands remaining and needing to maintain position, I should apply pressure with a continuation bet. My 9-Th has outs (overcards, straight draws) and can win the hand outright here. A 250 bet is appropriately sized to take down the pot without overcommitting chips.

I have middle pair (Fours) on a relatively low board. Claude's bet of 250 into 400 is a standard continuation bet that could be made with a wide range of hands, including overcards, draws, or air. My hand is strong enough to call one street to see a turn card, as folding here would be too exploitable. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn based on the card and Claude's action.

On the turn card 6d, the board is 3s-4h-8h-6d. My pair of fours is now third pair. Since I am out of position and the board is becoming more connected (any 5 or 7 now has straight potential), the best play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to see how Claude reacts and potentially realize my equity if he checks back, or decide whether to call a bet based on the sizing and my reads of his previous play style.

On the turn with 9-10 offsuit on 3-4-8-6 board. I have no made hand and roughly 14 outs (pair outs + straight outs). Flash has checked twice, suggesting weakness. Taking the free card to the river is optimal here - I maintain my stack while keeping equity alive. If I improve to a pair or straight on the river, I'll have a playable hand. If I whiff, I can evaluate the river action from a stronger information position. Betting again without a made hand is overcommitting with limited fold equity against an aggressive opponent who's shown strength in this session.

On the river card 7d, the board is 3s-4h-8h-6d-7d. I have a pair of Fours (Ts-4c). The board is quite connected, and a straight is possible with any 5 or a combination like 9-10 (I have the Ten, but not the Nine). Claude checked back on the turn, which often indicates a medium-strength hand or a missed draw. However, my pair of fours is very weak on this board and has little showdown value against a typical button-raising range. Betting is unlikely to fold out a better hand or get called by a worse one. Therefore, checking is the most prudent action to reach a cheap showdown or evaluate based on Claude's reaction.

River value bet with a made straight (6-7-8-9-10). Flash has checked three streets indicating weakness. A 500 chip bet represents a reasonable value bet that extracts chips from Flash's weak holdings while maintaining controlled risk. This sizing should get called by weaker hands like pairs or missed draws, allowing me to build my stack in the final hands of this session.

On a board of 3s-4h-8h-6d-7d, my pair of Fours (Ts-4c) is extremely weak. The board is very connected, with any 5 making a straight (3-4-5-6-7 or 4-5-6-7-8). Claude raised pre-flop and bet the flop, then checked the turn. His river bet of 500 into a 900 pot suggests he either has a piece of this board (an 8, 7, 6, or a 5) or is attempting to bluff with a missed draw (like a flush draw). Since my pair of Fours is currently the fourth-best pair on the board and loses to almost any pair Claude would realistically have in his range, it is not a strong enough hand to call a 500-chip bet. I have better hands in my range to call with, and at this stage of the match with a significant chip lead, there is no reason to make a high-risk hero call with bottom pair.

I am on the button with K9o. This is a standard opening hand in a heads-up situation. My stack is much larger than Claude's, but he has been playing fairly solid. Raising to 300 has been my consistent sizing and is effective for applying pressure while maintaining control of the pot. Opening here allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or see a flop in position.

Folding 3-9 offsuit facing a raise from Flash on the button. This is a marginal weak hand that lacks the strength needed to call out of position against an aggressive opponent. Flash has shown consistent raising patterns throughout the session, indicating a wide range. At this stage of the tournament (hand 140 of 150), I need to preserve chips and wait for premium holdings in better spots. The pot odds of 3:1 don't justify playing a hand with poor equity in position, especially when I could face significant post-flop pressure. Folding preserves my 12,300-chip stack for better opportunities in the remaining 10 hands.

6-3 offsuit is below the threshold for playing from the small blind, even with favorable pot odds of 3:1. Key considerations: (1) Out of position post-flop against the BB, (2) Weak hand that doesn't win frequently against BB's calling range, (3) With a 122.5 BB stack, I need to preserve chips for better opportunities, (4) Flash is aggressive and plays a wide range, so folding marginal hands keeps my win rate positive. The 50 chips I'd invest would likely result in losses on future streets given the position disadvantage. This is a textbook fold.

I am on the button with 82o, which is a weak starting hand. While I have been aggressive in recent hands, 82o is generally not worth playing even in a heads-up situation against a short-stacked opponent. Folding here preserves my lead and allows me to wait for a better spot in the final few hands.

7-4 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. While it only costs 50 more to call in heads-up play, I'm short-stacked with 12,250 chips (against Flash's 27,600). With only 8 hands remaining (we're at hand 143 of 150), I should be selective with my stack preservation. Even in heads-up, 7-4o doesn't have enough equity to justify calling, especially when short-stacked where every chip matters. I'll fold and wait for a premium hand where I can maximize my remaining stack.

I have Ad4d on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a strong starting hand for heads-up play, as it is a suited Ace. Standard strategy is to raise to put pressure on the big blind and build a pot with a range advantage. My stack is significantly larger than Claude's, allowing me to play aggressively. I will raise to the standard 3x big blind size (300 total) to stay consistent with my previous patterns and capitalize on my hand strength.

Flash is raising from the button in heads-up play. With A7o from the big blind and only 6 hands remaining, this is a reasonable holding to continue with. At 2:1 pot odds (200 to call, 400 in pot), A7o has sufficient equity to call and see the flop. The ace gives me high card potential and potential to flop top pair, while the seven adds some additional outs. Flash has shown a pattern of aggressive button raising but is somewhat exploitable post-flop, making this a profitable spot to continue and play post-flop. A call is the most appropriate action here.

Checking with A7o on JcJsQs. The board is heavily coordinated with paired jacks and a queen. I have only ace-high with limited outs. Being out of position and holding a marginal hand, checking is the prudent play. This keeps the pot manageable and allows me to see Flash's action before committing more chips. With 6 hands remaining and needing to play tight from bad positions, I should avoid putting chips in unnecessarily with such a weak holding on a dangerous board.

I am heads-up on a Jc-Js-Qs board after raising pre-flop with Ad4d. This board texture is quite connected and hits a lot of broadway cards that the big blind might call with, such as Queens, Jacks, or straight draws like K-T and T-9. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I have the range advantage and can represent a strong hand like a pocket pair higher than the board, an Ace-Queen, or even a Jack. Claude has checked to me, which is standard. I will place a continuation bet of half the pot (300) to put pressure on his marginal hands and potentially take the pot down right now. If he calls, I have backdoor nut-flush potential and an overcard to help me on future streets.

Calling the 300 raise with A7o on JcJsQs. At 3:1 pot odds, I need ~25% equity. Against Flash's aggressive button-raising range, my ace-high with 3 clean outs meets this threshold. With 6 hands remaining and 11,950 chips, the risk/reward is acceptable. This avoids overfolding to aggression while preserving the option to fold to subsequent aggression if I miss the turn. The pot is now 1200, and I'll have position information going to the turn.

Checking with A7o on JcJsQs9h. Flash's check on the turn indicates weakness and gives me a free look at the river. With only ace-high and limited equity, betting here is not profitable. Checking preserves chips while maintaining the possibility of winning the pot outright or improving on the river. With 7 hands remaining and a short stack, pot control and chip preservation are priorities over speculative betting with a weak hand.

The turn 9h makes the board extremely coordinated, completing potential straights (like K-T or T-8) and improving many hands in Claude's calling range (like T-9 or Q-9). Since my Ace-high has very little equity against a range that called a flop bet on J-J-Q, and because the board is now so dangerous, I will check behind to see a free river and avoid being check-raised off the hand.

River check with A7o on JcJsQs9hJd. This is ace-high only on a board with three jacks - an extremely weak holding. Betting won't win; it either folds out better hands or gets called by hands that beat ace-high. Flash's turn check suggests medium strength or a missed draw. In short-stack mode with 6 hands remaining and 11,650 chips, chip preservation is critical. Checking allows me to see the showdown without additional risk and may win outright if Flash was drawing.

The board has completed with a third Jack, giving me Three of a Kind with an Ace kicker (J-J-J-A-Q). While this is a strong hand, the board texture is extremely dangerous because any Queen or Nine in Claude's hand makes a Full House. Claude called a bet on the flop (J-J-Q), which heavily suggests he has a piece of this board, likely a Queen or a draw that may have hit. Since Claude checked the river, he may be looking to check-call with a marginal Full House or is simply giving up. Betting into him is risky because I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, while better hands (Qx, 99) will definitely call or raise. Checking behind allows me to get to showdown and win against his bluffs or weaker Ace-high hands.

2-5 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. While heads-up play widens ranges considerably and pot odds of 3:1 are mathematically tempting, I need to consider my position: I'm short-stacked at 12,200 chips versus Flash's 27,650, and we're in the critical endgame with only 6 hands remaining (145 of 150). Preserving my stack for premium hands is crucial at this stage. The risk-reward doesn't favor playing such a marginal hand when I could wait for better opportunities in the remaining hands.

I am on the button with Qs 9c. This is a solid opening hand in a heads-up situation. I have a significant chip lead over Claude with only 5 hands remaining in the game. My strategy is to continue playing standard aggressive poker from the button to maintain pressure. Claude has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop and post-flop aggression in recent hands (e.g., Hand #135 and Hand #139). I will open for my standard 3x raise of 300 chips to win the blinds or play a pot in position.

Folding 3c-8s from the Big Blind. This is a very weak unpaired hand with minimal equity. While we're late in the tournament (5 hands remaining) and shorter-stacked at 12.1k vs Flash's 27.5k, this specific hand doesn't warrant a call or aggression. Flash has shown consistent aggression throughout, and 3-8o has virtually no equity against his typical raising range from the button. With only a few hands left, preserving chips for a better spot is more valuable than trying to go to war with a dominated hand out of position. The risk-reward isn't favorable here.

Calling 50 chips to complete the small blind with TdJh in heads-up play. Excellent 3:1 pot odds, broadway connector with straight potential, and positional advantage post-flop. Definitely worth playing this hand.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of Fours (4c 4s). Claude, who is on the Button/Small Blind, has just completed (limped) the blind. Pocket fours are a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation, but their value primarily comes from either hitting a set or being played aggressively pre-flop to take down the pot or define the range. Since the opponent limped, I want to build a pot and take control of the hand. Raising here is standard to charge the limper and potentially end the hand immediately. I will raise to 300 total.

With only 4 hands remaining, I'm in a critical spot with 12,000 chips against Flash's 27,600. I hold Td Jh (broadway connector) on the button with 2:1 pot odds to call 200 more. This is a solid hand that can make straights and has reasonable high-card strength. Key factors: (1) Good position on the button gives me a significant post-flop advantage in heads-up play, (2) Flash has been extremely aggressive throughout recent hands, suggesting weakness/exploitation of position rather than strong hands, (3) Pot odds of 2:1 are acceptable for a hand with this much equity, (4) With limited chips remaining and few hands left, I need to accumulate chips by winning pots, and folding here means ceding initiative. The call allows me to see the flop in position with a playable hand where I can exercise positional control post-flop.

On this coordinated board of 9h-Qh-8d, my pocket fours are an underpair and significantly behind many hands in Claude's range, especially since he called my pre-flop raise. This board offers many straight and flush draws, making it likely he has a piece of it. As the first to act, I will check to see how he responds, aiming to keep the pot small and potentially see a turn for free, as my hand has very little showdown value here.

With only 4 hands remaining and critically short-stacked (11,800 vs 27,600), I need to accumulate chips. I hold Td-Jh on a 9h-Qh-8d flop - a strong drawing hand with roughly 13 outs (10 hearts for a flush draw, 4 kings for a straight draw with minimal overlap). Flash checked, suggesting possible weakness or a trap, but regardless I should leverage my position and equity. A bet of 300 (half pot) accomplishes several goals: (1) builds the pot when I have equity, (2) takes down the 600 pot if Flash folds, (3) gains information about my opponent's hand strength, and (4) maintains aggression when short-stacked. The broadway cards give me multiple ways to improve, and in heads-up play with position, betting a drawing hand with this much equity is standard. Checking would surrender initiative and allow Flash to control the narrative on future streets.

The board of 9h-Qh-8d is highly coordinated and connects extremely well with Claude's range after calling a pre-flop raise. My pocket fours are now an underpair and are likely behind. Claude's half-pot bet indicates strength or a high-equity draw. Given the many straight and flush possibilities, my hand has very little showdown value and only two outs to improve. Folding is the safest and most strategic play to preserve my stack for the final hands.

In heads-up play, the button should open a wide range of hands, but Nine-Four offsuit is extremely weak and falls into the bottom tier of starting hands. Considering my significant stack lead and the fact that Claude has been defending his big blind reasonably well lately, I will choose to fold this hand to avoid playing a weak hand out of position post-flop or facing a three-bet.

Pocket jacks is a premium hand, especially in heads-up play. I'm on the button with position advantage. Down in chips, I need to play aggressively with strong hands to accumulate the chip stack needed for the final hand. Raising to 300 applies pressure, builds the pot, and gives me the best chance to win chips. This is a standard aggressive play with JJ - I can win the pot immediately or get value if Flash calls with a weaker holding.

I am in the big blind with Ten of Hearts and Nine of Diamonds. Claude, the small blind, has raised to 300. My hand is a decent connected offsuit hand that plays well enough in a heads-up pot. Given the 2:1 odds on a call and the fact that we are heads-up, defending the big blind here is standard. Claude has been relatively active, and while I have a significant chip lead, T9o is strong enough to see a flop.

I am heads-up in the Big Blind with Ten of Hearts and Nine of Diamonds. On a board of King-Jack-Deuce, I have a gutshot straight draw. Since I am acting first on the flop and this board texture favors the pre-flop raiser's range, I will check to see how Claude proceeds. Betting into a range that contains many Kings and Jacks is not ideal here, and checking allows me to potentially see a free card or evaluate based on the size of a continuation bet.

With three of a kind (flopped set of jacks) on a safe board, this is a premium hand. Flash checked, indicating weakness. Down in chips with only 2 hands left, I need to build my stack. A 450 bet is appropriately aggressive - it builds the pot for value, applies pressure, and should elicit a response that helps me understand Flash's hand strength. The Kc doesn't hurt my hand given Flash's check, and this is an excellent spot to win chips.

On a board of Jack-King-Deuce, I am holding T9o, which gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen). Claude, the pre-flop raiser, has bet 450 into a pot of 600. The pot odds are 450 to win 1050, meaning I need about 30% equity to call. A gutshot only hits about 8.5% of the time on the turn. Given that this board texture strongly favors the pre-flop raiser's range (containing many Kings and Jacks) and I have no other significant pieces of the board, the call is mathematically incorrect. Furthermore, we are on hand 149 of 150, and I have a significant stack lead. There is no reason to take a high-variance, negative-EV risk in this spot. I will fold and preserve my chips for the final hand.

I am holding 54o in the small blind on the final hand of the session (150/150). I currently hold a significant lead over Claude (27,200 to 12,650). Since my goal is to maximize my stack and conclude the game with this lead intact, there is no strategic reason to enter a pot with a very weak hand like 54o. Folding is the lowest-variance move to secure my current standing.
